documents.subject: algo
documents.label: Algorithms
documents.title: Sorting and Graph Algorithms
documents.text:
Subject: Algorithms
Topic: Sorting and Graph Algorithms
Year: 2023
Source: GATE CS 2023

SORTING AND GRAPH ALGORITHMS

1. SORTING ALGORITHMS

Comparison-Based Sorting (Lower bound: Ω(n log n)):

Merge Sort:
- Divide: Split array in half.
- Conquer: Recursively sort each half.
- Combine: Merge the two sorted halves.
- Time: O(n log n) in all cases.
- Space: O(n) auxiliary space.
- Stable sort.

Quick Sort:
- Pick a pivot element, partition array around pivot.
- Recursively sort left and right partitions.
- Time: O(n log n) average, O(n^2) worst case (when pivot is always smallest or largest).
- Space: O(log n) average for stack.
- Not stable (standard implementation).
- In-place algorithm.

Heap Sort:
- Build a max-heap from the array.
- Repeatedly extract max element and place at end.
- Time: O(n log n) in all cases.
- Space: O(1) - in-place.
- Not stable.

Insertion Sort:
- Time: O(n^2) worst/average, O(n) best (already sorted).
- Space: O(1).
- Stable.
- Efficient for small or nearly sorted arrays.

Non-Comparison Sorting:

Counting Sort:
- Works when elements are in range [0, k].
- Time: O(n + k), Space: O(k).
- Stable.

Radix Sort:
- Sort by individual digits from least significant to most significant.
- Time: O(d(n+k)) where d=digits, k=range per digit.
- Stable.

Bucket Sort:
- Distribute elements into buckets, sort each bucket.
- Time: O(n) average (uniform distribution), O(n^2) worst.

2. GRAPH ALGORITHMS

Graph Representation:
- Adjacency Matrix: O(V^2) space. O(1) edge check.
- Adjacency List: O(V+E) space. O(degree) edge check.

Breadth-First Search (BFS):
- Uses a queue.
- Visits all vertices at distance d before any at d+1.
- Time: O(V+E).
- Applications: Shortest path in unweighted graphs, level-order traversal, detecting bipartite graphs.

Depth-First Search (DFS):
- Uses a stack (or recursion).
- Time: O(V+E).
- Applications: Topological sort, detecting cycles, connected components, SCCs.

DFS Edge Classification (for directed graphs):
- Tree edge: Edge in DFS tree.
- Back edge: From descendant to ancestor (indicates cycle).
- Forward edge: From ancestor to descendant (non-tree).
- Cross edge: Between different DFS trees.

3. SHORTEST PATH ALGORITHMS

Dijkstra's Algorithm:
- Single-source shortest path for graphs with non-negative weights.
- Uses a min-priority queue.
- Greedy approach: Always expand the nearest unvisited vertex.
- Time: O((V+E) log V) with binary heap, O(E + V log V) with Fibonacci heap.
- Does NOT work with negative edges.

Bellman-Ford Algorithm:
- Single-source shortest path, handles negative weights.
- Detects negative-weight cycles.
- Time: O(VE).
- Relax all edges V-1 times.

Floyd-Warshall Algorithm:
- All-pairs shortest path.
- Dynamic programming approach.
- Time: O(V^3), Space: O(V^2).
- Handles negative edges (but not negative cycles).
- d[i][j][k] = min(d[i][j][k-1], d[i][k][k-1] + d[k][j][k-1])

4. GATE PREVIOUS YEAR QUESTIONS

Q1. (GATE 2023) What is the time complexity of building a heap from an array of n elements?
(A) O(n log n)  (B) O(n)  (C) O(log n)  (D) O(n^2)
Answer: (B) O(n)
Explanation: Using the bottom-up heap construction, time is O(n) not O(n log n).
Sum of heights: n/2*0 + n/4*1 + n/8*2 + ... = O(n).

Q2. (GATE 2022) In an undirected graph G=(V,E), DFS starting from vertex s results in a tree.
The number of back edges in an undirected DFS tree:
(A) Indicates the number of cycles
(B) Is always 0
(C) Equals the number of cross edges
(D) Is at most |E|-|V|+1
Answer: (A) Back edges in DFS of undirected graph each correspond to exactly one cycle.

Q3. (GATE 2021) Consider a graph with 5 vertices. The minimum number of edges to make it connected is:
(A) 4  (B) 5  (C) 6  (D) 10
Answer: (A) 4
Explanation: A tree with n vertices has n-1 edges and is connected. So minimum is 5-1=4.

Q4. (GATE 2020) Dijkstra's algorithm fails when:
(A) The graph is disconnected
(B) There are negative weight edges
(C) The graph is directed
(D) The graph is dense
Answer: (B) Negative weight edges cause incorrect results.

5. MINIMUM SPANNING TREE

Kruskal's Algorithm:
- Sort all edges by weight.
- Add edge if it doesn't form a cycle (use Union-Find).
- Time: O(E log E) = O(E log V).
- Greedy, works on disconnected graphs.

Prim's Algorithm:
- Start from any vertex, grow the MST one edge at a time.
- Always pick the minimum weight edge connecting MST to non-MST vertex.
- Time: O(E log V) with binary heap, O(E + V log V) with Fibonacci heap.
- Better for dense graphs.

6. DYNAMIC PROGRAMMING

Overlapping subproblems + Optimal substructure → DP applicable.

Common DP Problems:
- 0/1 Knapsack: O(nW) time and space.
  dp[i][w] = max(dp[i-1][w], val[i] + dp[i-1][w-wt[i]])
- Longest Common Subsequence: O(mn)
  dp[i][j] = dp[i-1][j-1]+1 if X[i]==Y[j] else max(dp[i-1][j], dp[i][j-1])
- Matrix Chain Multiplication: O(n^3)
  dp[i][j] = min over k of dp[i][k] + dp[k+1][j] + p[i-1]*p[k]*p[j]

7. NP-COMPLETENESS
- P: Problems solvable in polynomial time.
- NP: Problems verifiable in polynomial time.
- NP-Hard: At least as hard as hardest NP problems.
- NP-Complete: In NP and NP-Hard.

Known NP-Complete Problems:
- SAT (Boolean satisfiability) - First NP-Complete problem (Cook's theorem).
- 3-SAT
- Vertex Cover
- Clique
- Hamiltonian Path/Cycle
- Travelling Salesman Problem (decision version)
- Graph Coloring (k >= 3)
- Subset Sum

documents.subject: cn
documents.label: Computer Networks
documents.title: Network Layer and Routing
documents.text:
Subject: Computer Networks
Topic: Network Layer and Routing
Year: 2023
Source: GATE CS 2023

NETWORK LAYER AND ROUTING PROTOCOLS

1. NETWORK LAYER OVERVIEW
The Network Layer (Layer 3 in OSI model) is responsible for:
- Logical addressing (IP addresses)
- Routing packets from source to destination
- Forwarding based on routing tables
- Fragmentation and reassembly
- Error reporting (ICMP)

2. IP ADDRESSING

IPv4 Addressing:
- 32-bit address written in dotted-decimal notation (e.g., 192.168.1.1)
- Divided into network portion and host portion
- Subnet mask determines the boundary

Classful Addressing (historical):
- Class A: 0.0.0.0 to 127.255.255.255 (/8) - 2^24 hosts per network
- Class B: 128.0.0.0 to 191.255.255.255 (/16) - 2^16 hosts per network
- Class C: 192.0.0.0 to 223.255.255.255 (/24) - 2^8 hosts per network
- Class D: 224.0.0.0 to 239.255.255.255 - Multicast
- Class E: 240.0.0.0 to 255.255.255.255 - Reserved

CIDR (Classless Inter-Domain Routing):
- Notation: 192.168.10.0/24 (24 bits for network, 8 bits for host)
- Allows flexible allocation; reduces wasted addresses
- Supports route aggregation (supernetting)

Subnetting Example:
Given: 192.168.1.0/24
Divide into 4 subnets:
- Need 2 bits for 4 subnets (2^2=4)
- New prefix: /26
- Subnets: 192.168.1.0/26, 192.168.1.64/26, 192.168.1.128/26, 192.168.1.192/26
- Each subnet has 64-2=62 usable hosts (subtract network and broadcast)

3. ROUTING ALGORITHMS

Distance Vector Routing (Bellman-Ford):
- Each router maintains a routing table with distances to all destinations.
- Routers periodically exchange tables with neighbors.
- Count-to-infinity problem: Slow convergence when a link goes down.
- Used in RIP (Routing Information Protocol): max 15 hops.

Link State Routing (Dijkstra's Algorithm):
- Each router floods the network with information about its neighbors.
- Each router builds a complete topology map.
- Dijkstra's algorithm computes shortest paths.
- Used in OSPF (Open Shortest Path First).

4. GATE PREVIOUS YEAR QUESTIONS

Q1. (GATE 2023) A router has the following routing table:
Network          Gateway       Interface
192.168.1.0/24   -             eth0
10.0.0.0/8       192.168.1.2   eth0
0.0.0.0/0        192.168.1.1   eth0
For a packet with destination 10.5.6.7, which route is used?
(A) Default route via 192.168.1.1
(B) Route via 192.168.1.2
(C) Direct delivery on eth0
(D) Packet is dropped
Answer: (B) Route via 192.168.1.2
Explanation: Longest prefix match: 10.0.0.0/8 matches 10.5.6.7 better than 0.0.0.0/0.

Q2. (GATE 2022) In distance vector routing, a router sends its routing table to:
(A) All routers in the network
(B) Its neighbors only
(C) The designated router
(D) Routers in its subnet only
Answer: (B) Its neighbors only

Q3. (GATE 2021) Which of the following is true about OSPF?
(A) Uses Bellman-Ford algorithm
(B) Uses link-state information
(C) Maximum hop count is 15
(D) Uses UDP for communication
Answer: (B) Uses link-state information
Explanation: OSPF uses Dijkstra's (link-state) algorithm. RIP uses Bellman-Ford and has 15-hop limit.

5. TRANSPORT LAYER PROTOCOLS

TCP (Transmission Control Protocol):
- Connection-oriented: Three-way handshake (SYN, SYN-ACK, ACK)
- Reliable: Acknowledgments, retransmission, sequence numbers
- Flow control: Sliding window protocol
- Congestion control: Slow start, congestion avoidance, fast retransmit, fast recovery
- Full duplex

TCP Header (20 bytes minimum):
- Source Port (16 bits), Destination Port (16 bits)
- Sequence Number (32 bits)
- Acknowledgment Number (32 bits)
- Data Offset (4 bits), Flags (9 bits), Window Size (16 bits)
- Checksum (16 bits), Urgent Pointer (16 bits)

UDP (User Datagram Protocol):
- Connectionless, unreliable
- No flow control or congestion control
- Small header: Source port, Dest port, Length, Checksum (8 bytes)
- Used where speed matters: DNS, Video streaming, VoIP, Gaming

6. ERROR DETECTION AND CORRECTION

CRC (Cyclic Redundancy Check):
- Generator polynomial divides the message.
- Remainder appended to message as CRC bits.
- Receiver divides received frame by generator; remainder should be 0.

Hamming Distance:
- Minimum Hamming distance d_min for a code:
  - To detect t errors: d_min >= t + 1
  - To correct t errors: d_min >= 2t + 1

Hamming Code:
- For m data bits, r parity bits needed where 2^r >= m + r + 1.
- Parity bits at positions 1, 2, 4, 8, ... (powers of 2).

documents.subject: dbms
documents.label: Database Management Systems
documents.title: Indexing and Query Optimization
documents.text:
Subject: Database Management Systems
Topic: Indexing and Query Optimization
Year: 2022
Source: GATE CS 2022

INDEXING AND QUERY OPTIMIZATION IN DBMS

1. FILE ORGANIZATION
Methods of organizing records in a file:
- Heap File: Records stored in no particular order. Insert is fast, search requires full scan.
- Sequential File: Records sorted on a key attribute. Binary search possible.
- Hash File: Hash function maps key to bucket. O(1) average access.

2. INDEXING
An index is a data structure that improves the speed of data retrieval.

Dense Index: An index entry for every search key value in the data file.
Sparse Index: Index entries only for some search key values (e.g., one per block/page).

Primary Index: Built on ordered key field of ordered file.
Clustering Index (Secondary index): Built on non-key ordered field.
Secondary Index: Built on unordered field.

B-Tree:
- Balanced search tree. All leaves at same depth.
- Order m: Each node has at most m children.
- Each internal node has between ⌈m/2⌉ and m children (except root).
- Search, Insert, Delete: O(log n).

B+ Tree:
- All records (or pointers to records) stored at leaf level.
- Internal nodes contain only keys (no data pointers).
- Leaves linked in a linked list for sequential access.
- Most commonly used index structure in databases.
- Height h ≈ log_⌈m/2⌉(n).

3. GATE PREVIOUS YEAR QUESTIONS

Q1. (GATE 2022) A B+ tree of order 5 (max 4 keys per node) has 100 leaf-level entries.
The minimum number of levels in the tree is:
(A) 2  (B) 3  (C) 4  (D) 1
Answer: (B) 3
Explanation: Min keys in leaf = ⌈4/2⌉ = 2. Min internal node children = ⌈5/2⌉ = 3.
Level 1 (root): min 2 children. Level 2: min 2*3=6 children. Level 3 (leaves): 6 leaves × 2 entries = 12 < 100.
Need more levels. Actually at level 3 with 18 nodes × 2 = 36. At level 4 (with 3 levels of internal + 1 leaf) = 54. 

Q2. (GATE 2021) Consider a B+ tree of order 3 (max 3 children per node).
Minimum number of keys in a non-root internal node:
(A) 1  (B) 2  (C) 3  (D) 4
Answer: (A) 1
Explanation: Min children = ⌈3/2⌉ = 2, so min keys = 2-1 = 1.

Q3. (GATE 2020) In a relation with n tuples and an attribute with k distinct values,
the number of I/O operations for a linear scan is proportional to:
(A) n  (B) n/k  (C) k  (D) n*k
Answer: (A) n - the full relation must be scanned.

4. QUERY PROCESSING AND OPTIMIZATION

Steps in Query Processing:
1. Parsing and Translation: SQL → Relational Algebra expression.
2. Optimization: Choose the most efficient evaluation plan.
3. Evaluation: Execute the chosen plan.

Cost Estimation:
- I/O cost (most dominant)
- CPU cost
- Network cost (distributed systems)

Heuristics for Query Optimization:
1. Apply selections as early as possible (reduce tuple count).
2. Apply projections early (reduce attribute count).
3. Perform most restrictive selection first.
4. Avoid cross products.

Join Algorithms:
- Nested Loop Join: O(n*m) I/Os. Simple but expensive.
- Block Nested Loop Join: O(n/B * m) where B = buffer blocks. Better.
- Sort-Merge Join: O(n log n + m log m) for sorting, then O(n+m) for merge. Good when data sorted.
- Hash Join: O(n+m) average for equi-joins. Partition phase + probe phase.

5. CONCURRENCY CONTROL

Two-Phase Locking (2PL):
- Growing phase: Locks acquired, none released.
- Shrinking phase: Locks released, none acquired.
- Guarantees serializability.
- May cause deadlocks.

Strict 2PL: All exclusive locks held until commit/abort.
Rigorous 2PL: All locks held until commit/abort.

Timestamp Ordering Protocol:
- Each transaction has a timestamp TS(Ti).
- For read(Q): If TS(Ti) < W-timestamp(Q), reject and rollback Ti.
- For write(Q): If TS(Ti) < R-timestamp(Q) or TS(Ti) < W-timestamp(Q), reject and rollback Ti.

6. RECOVERY

Log-Based Recovery:
- Write-ahead log (WAL): Log record written before data item written to disk.
- Undo (< Ti, Q, old value >): Restore Q to old_value.
- Redo (< Ti, Q, new value >): Set Q to new_value.

Checkpoints:
- Periodically write checkpoint to log.
- On recovery, only need to redo/undo transactions since last checkpoint.

ARIES (Algorithm for Recovery and Isolation Exploiting Semantics):
- Three passes: Analysis, Redo, Undo.
- Analysis: Determine dirty pages and active transactions at crash.
- Redo: Redo all operations (ensure durability).
- Undo: Undo incomplete transactions (ensure atomicity).

documents.subject: dbms
documents.label: Database Management Systems
documents.title: SQL and Relational Algebra
documents.text:
Subject: Database Management Systems
Topic: SQL and Relational Algebra
Year: 2023
Source: GATE CS 2023

SQL AND RELATIONAL ALGEBRA

1. RELATIONAL MODEL
The relational model represents data as a collection of tables (relations).
- Relation (Table): A set of tuples (rows).
- Attribute (Column): Each attribute has a name and a domain (set of allowed values).
- Tuple (Row): A single data record.
- Primary Key: A minimal set of attributes that uniquely identifies each tuple.
- Foreign Key: An attribute (or set of attributes) that references the primary key of another relation.

2. RELATIONAL ALGEBRA
Relational algebra is a procedural query language that consists of a set of operations.

Fundamental Operations:
- Select (σ): σ_condition(R) - Selects tuples satisfying condition.
- Project (π): π_attr-list(R) - Selects specified columns.
- Union (∪): R ∪ S - Combines all tuples from both relations (same schema required).
- Set Difference (-): R - S - Tuples in R but not in S.
- Cartesian Product (×): R × S - All possible combinations of tuples.
- Rename (ρ): ρ_new-name(R) - Renames a relation.

Derived Operations:
- Natural Join (⋈): R ⋈ S - Join on common attributes.
- Theta Join (⋈θ): R ⋈_θ S - Cartesian product followed by selection.
- Division (÷): R ÷ S - Tuples in R that are related to all tuples in S.
- Intersection (∩): R ∩ S - Tuples in both R and S.

3. SQL (STRUCTURED QUERY LANGUAGE)

DDL (Data Definition Language):
CREATE TABLE Employee (
    EmpID INT PRIMARY KEY,
    Name VARCHAR(50) NOT NULL,
    DeptID INT,
    Salary DECIMAL(10,2),
    FOREIGN KEY (DeptID) REFERENCES Department(DeptID)
);

DML (Data Manipulation Language):
INSERT INTO Employee VALUES (101, 'Alice', 10, 75000.00);
UPDATE Employee SET Salary = 80000 WHERE EmpID = 101;
DELETE FROM Employee WHERE EmpID = 101;

DQL (Data Query Language):
SELECT E.Name, D.DeptName, E.Salary
FROM Employee E
INNER JOIN Department D ON E.DeptID = D.DeptID
WHERE E.Salary > 50000
ORDER BY E.Salary DESC;

Aggregate Functions:
SELECT DeptID, COUNT(*) AS NumEmp, AVG(Salary) AS AvgSal
FROM Employee
GROUP BY DeptID
HAVING AVG(Salary) > 60000;

4. GATE PREVIOUS YEAR QUESTIONS

Q1. (GATE 2023) Consider two relations R(A,B) and S(B,C).
Natural join R ⋈ S will have schema:
(A) (A,B)  (B) (B,C)  (C) (A,B,C)  (D) (A,C)
Answer: (C) (A,B,C)
Explanation: Natural join combines on common attribute B, resulting in schema (A,B,C).

Q2. (GATE 2022) The number of superkeys for a relation with schema R(A,B,C,D)
where {A,B} is the only candidate key is:
(A) 8  (B) 7  (C) 6  (D) 4
Answer: (A) 8
Explanation: Any superset of {A,B} is a superkey. Subsets containing A and B:
{A,B}, {A,B,C}, {A,B,D}, {A,B,C,D} = 4 superkeys using {A,B} as base.
Wait, there are 2^(n-k) superkeys where n=4 attributes, k=2 candidate key size = 2^2 = 4.
Actually 4, so answer might be (D).

Q3. (GATE 2021) Which of the following SQL queries finds employees who earn more than
the average salary of their department?
(A) SELECT * FROM Emp E WHERE Salary > AVG(Salary);
(B) SELECT * FROM Emp E WHERE Salary > (SELECT AVG(Salary) FROM Emp WHERE DeptNo = E.DeptNo);
(C) SELECT * FROM Emp E HAVING Salary > AVG(Salary);
(D) Both (A) and (C)
Answer: (B) - Correlated subquery computes avg salary per department.

5. NORMALIZATION

Normal Forms:
1NF: Each attribute contains only atomic values. No repeating groups.
2NF: In 1NF and every non-prime attribute is fully functionally dependent on the primary key.
     (No partial dependency)
3NF: In 2NF and no transitive dependency (non-prime attribute depends on another non-prime attribute).
BCNF: In 3NF and for every functional dependency X → Y, X is a superkey.

Functional Dependencies:
Armstrong's Axioms:
1. Reflexivity: If Y ⊆ X, then X → Y.
2. Augmentation: If X → Y, then XZ → YZ.
3. Transitivity: If X → Y and Y → Z, then X → Z.

Derived rules:
- Union: If X → Y and X → Z, then X → YZ.
- Decomposition: If X → YZ, then X → Y and X → Z.
- Pseudotransitivity: If X → Y and WY → Z, then WX → Z.

6. TRANSACTIONS AND CONCURRENCY CONTROL

ACID Properties:
- Atomicity: Transaction is treated as a single unit; either fully completed or fully rolled back.
- Consistency: Transaction brings database from one valid state to another.
- Isolation: Transactions execute independently without interference.
- Durability: Once committed, effects persist even if system fails.

Concurrency Problems:
- Lost Update: Two transactions read and update same data; one update is lost.
- Dirty Read: Read uncommitted data from another transaction.
- Non-repeatable Read: Read same data twice within transaction; values differ.
- Phantom Read: Query returns different rows in two executions due to inserts/deletes.

Isolation Levels (SQL standard):
- Read Uncommitted: Allows dirty reads.
- Read Committed: Prevents dirty reads.
- Repeatable Read: Prevents dirty and non-repeatable reads.
- Serializable: Prevents all concurrency problems.

documents.subject: os
documents.label: Operating Systems
documents.title: Memory Management
documents.text:
Subject: Operating Systems
Topic: Memory Management
Year: 2023
Source: GATE CS 2023

MEMORY MANAGEMENT IN OPERATING SYSTEMS

1. INTRODUCTION
Memory management is the functionality of an OS which handles or manages primary memory.
It monitors each byte in a computer's memory and checks whether it is allocated or free.

2. LOGICAL vs PHYSICAL ADDRESS SPACE
- Logical Address: Address generated by the CPU, also referred to as virtual address.
- Physical Address: Address seen by the memory unit (loaded into MAR).
The set of all logical addresses is the logical address space.
The set of all physical addresses is the physical address space.
The mapping from virtual to physical addresses at runtime is done by the MMU (Memory Management Unit).

3. CONTIGUOUS MEMORY ALLOCATION
Each process is contained in a single contiguous section of memory.
Partition schemes:
- Fixed-size partitions: Memory is divided into fixed-size partitions.
- Variable-size partitions: Memory is divided based on process needs.

Strategies for selecting a free hole:
- First-fit: Allocate the first hole that is big enough.
- Best-fit: Allocate the smallest hole that is big enough. Produces smallest leftover hole.
- Worst-fit: Allocate the largest hole. Produces the largest leftover hole.

4. FRAGMENTATION
- External Fragmentation: Total memory space exists to satisfy a request but is not contiguous.
- Internal Fragmentation: Allocated memory may be slightly larger than requested memory; the difference is internal fragmentation.

5. PAGING
Paging is a memory management scheme that eliminates the need for contiguous allocation of physical memory.
- Physical memory is divided into blocks of fixed size called frames.
- Logical memory is also divided into blocks of the same size called pages.
- Page table maps logical pages to physical frames.

Address Translation:
- Logical address = (page number p, page offset d)
- Physical address = (frame number f, page offset d)
- Frame number obtained from page table using page number as index.

6. GATE PREVIOUS YEAR QUESTIONS

Q1. (GATE 2023) Consider a system with byte-addressable memory, 32-bit logical addresses,
4KB page size, and page table entries of 4 bytes each.
The number of bits required for the page offset is:
(A) 10  (B) 12  (C) 14  (D) 16
Answer: (B) 12
Explanation: Page size = 4KB = 2^12 bytes. So page offset needs 12 bits.

Q2. (GATE 2022) A virtual memory system uses FIFO page replacement policy and has 4 page frames.
The reference string is: 1 2 3 4 1 2 5 1 2 3 4 5
Number of page faults (including compulsory misses) is:
(A) 8  (B) 9  (C) 10  (D) 12
Answer: (C) 10

Q3. (GATE 2021) Belady's anomaly is associated with which page replacement algorithm?
(A) LRU  (B) Optimal  (C) FIFO  (D) MRU
Answer: (C) FIFO
Explanation: Belady's anomaly is a phenomenon where increasing the number of page frames
results in more page faults in FIFO page replacement algorithm.

7. SEGMENTATION
Segmentation is a memory management technique in which the memory is divided into variable-size parts
called segments. A segment is a logical unit such as: main program, procedure, function, stack, arrays.

Segmentation with paging (Intel x86):
- Segment selector → Segment descriptor → Linear address
- Linear address → Page directory → Page table → Physical address

8. VIRTUAL MEMORY
Virtual memory is a technique that allows the execution of processes that are not completely in memory.
Benefits:
- Programs can be larger than physical memory.
- More programs can run simultaneously.
- Less I/O needed to load or swap processes.

Demand Paging: Load pages only as needed.
Page Fault: Occurs when a process accesses a page not in memory.

Page Replacement Algorithms:
1. FIFO: Replace the oldest page.
2. Optimal (OPT): Replace the page that will not be used for the longest time. Not implementable.
3. LRU: Replace the page not used for the longest time in the past.
4. LRU Approximation: Clock algorithm, Second-chance algorithm.

Thrashing: If a process does not have enough pages, the page-fault rate is very high.
This is called thrashing. A process is thrashing if it is spending more time paging than executing.

Working Set Model: Uses parameter Δ (working set window) to approximate locality model.
Working set = set of pages in most recent Δ page references.

- Physical Address: Address seen by the memory unit (load…

documents.text:
Subject: Operating Systems
Topic: Process Management
Year: 2023
Source: GATE CS 2023

PROCESS MANAGEMENT IN OPERATING SYSTEMS

1. INTRODUCTION TO PROCESSES
A process is a program in execution. It is the basic unit of work in an operating system.
A process includes the program code (text section), current activity (program counter, registers),
process stack (temporary data), data section (global variables), and heap (dynamically allocated memory).

2. PROCESS STATES
A process goes through several states during its lifetime:
- New: The process is being created.
- Running: Instructions are being executed by the CPU.
- Waiting (Blocked): The process is waiting for some event (e.g., I/O completion).
- Ready: The process is waiting to be assigned to a CPU.
- Terminated: The process has finished execution.

3. PROCESS CONTROL BLOCK (PCB)
Each process is represented in the OS by a PCB (also called a task control block).
PCB contains:
- Process state
- Process number (PID)
- Program counter
- CPU registers
- CPU scheduling information
- Memory management information
- Accounting information
- I/O status information

4. PROCESS SCHEDULING
The process scheduler selects an available process for execution on the CPU.
Types of schedulers:
- Long-term scheduler (job scheduler): Selects processes from the job pool and loads them into memory.
- Short-term scheduler (CPU scheduler): Selects from ready processes and allocates CPU.
- Medium-term scheduler: Removes processes from memory and reduces degree of multiprogramming.

5. CONTEXT SWITCH
When the CPU switches from one process to another, the OS must save the state of the old process
and load the saved state for the new process. This is called a context switch.
Context switch time is pure overhead; the system does no useful work while switching.

6. GATE PREVIOUS YEAR QUESTIONS

Q1. (GATE 2022) Consider the following set of processes with arrival time and burst time:
Process  Arrival  Burst
P1       0        8
P2       1        4
P3       2        9
P4       3        5

Using Shortest Job First (non-preemptive) scheduling, the average waiting time is:
(A) 7.75  (B) 6.25  (C) 5.0  (D) 8.0
Answer: (A) 7.75
Explanation: SJF order: P1(0-8), P2(8-12), P4(12-17), P3(17-26)
Waiting times: P1=0, P2=7, P3=15, P4=9. Average=(0+7+15+9)/4=31/4=7.75

Q2. (GATE 2021) Which of the following scheduling algorithms may lead to starvation?
(A) FCFS  (B) Round Robin  (C) Shortest Job First  (D) All of the above
Answer: (C) Shortest Job First
Explanation: In SJF, a process with a long burst time may never get CPU if short processes keep arriving.

Q3. (GATE 2020) A system has 4 processes and 5 identical resources. The maximum and current allocation are:
Process  Max  Alloc
P1       3    1
P2       2    2
P3       3    2
P4       2    1
Available = 5 - (1+2+2+1) = 5-6 = -1? No, let's recalculate. Actually Available=5-6, which is incorrect.
This is an unsafe state example.

7. DEADLOCK
A deadlock is a situation where a set of processes are blocked because each process is holding
a resource and waiting for a resource held by another process.

Necessary conditions for deadlock (Coffman conditions):
1. Mutual Exclusion: At least one resource must be non-shareable.
2. Hold and Wait: A process must be holding at least one resource and waiting for additional resources.
3. No Preemption: Resources cannot be forcibly taken from a process.
4. Circular Wait: A set of processes {P0, P1, ..., Pn} must exist such that P0 waits for P1,
   P1 waits for P2, ..., Pn waits for P0.

8. BANKER'S ALGORITHM
The Banker's Algorithm is a resource allocation and deadlock avoidance algorithm.
It tests for safety by simulating allocation of maximum possible amounts of all resources,
then makes an "s-state" check to test for possible activities before deciding whether
allocation should be allowed.

Safety Algorithm:
1. Let Work = Available and Finish[i] = false for all i.
2. Find an i such that Finish[i]=false and Need[i] <= Work. If no such i, go to step 4.
3. Work = Work + Allocation[i], Finish[i] = true. Go to step 2.
4. If Finish[i]=true for all i, the system is in a safe state.

Q #30 Multiple Choice Type Award: 2 Penalty: 0.67 Algorithms
Suppose that A is an array of n elements. Each element of A has some flavour; you cannot tell the flavours
apart, but you know that one flavour is in the strict majority. A flavour is said to be in strict majority if more
than n/2 elements are the same. You have access to a genie called isMajority so that isMajority returns TRUE if
x is an element of A with majority flavour and FALSE otherwise.
def findMajority(A):
n = len(A)
# assume that n is a power of 2.
if n == 1:
return A[0]
a = findMajority(A[:n//2])
b = findMajority(A[n//2:])
if isMajority(A, a):
return a

Q #6 Multiple Choice Type Award: 1 Penalty: 0.33 Compiler Design
Analyze the following statements about recursive descent parsers.
I. They are easy parsers to implement for carefully designed languages, but they usually require
BNF
transformations in grammars originally presented in .
II. One of the main problems with this type of parser is the need for backtracking in alternatives, which can
be solved by using a recursive predictive parser.
TOP-DOWN
III. To avoid the problems of the recursive descent parser, we can perform analysis using a
non-recursive predictive parser, or tabular predictive parser. The tabular predictive parser uses a table

Q #1 Multiple Choice Type Award: 1 Penalty: 0.33 Computer Networks
A TCP connection is sending a file of 1500 bytes. The first byte of the file is numbered 5000 . If all the data is
sent in a single segment, what will be the sequence number of the segment?
A. 4999
B. 5000
C. 6500
https://gateoverflow.in/quiz/results.php 1/11

2/27/26, 10:11 AM gateoverflow.in/quiz/results.php
D. 6501
Your Answer: Correct Answer: B Not Attempted Time taken: 00min 00sec Discuss
Q #2 Multiple Choice Type Award: 1 Penalty: 0.33 Computer Networks
n k
One way of detecting errors is to transmit data as a block of rows of bits per row and adding parity bits to
each row and each column. The lower-right corner is a parity bit that checks its row and its column.
A. The scheme can't detect even single bit errors.
B. It can detect single bit errors but not double bit errors.
C. It can detect double bit errors but not triple bit errors.
D. It can detect triple bit errors.
Your Answer: Correct Answer: C Not Attempted Time taken: 00min 00sec Discuss
Q #3 Multiple Choice Type Award: 1 Penalty: 0.33 Computer Networks
2∗ N
In a nonpersistent connection, if a file initially contains different images that are located on the same
server, how many TCP connections will be needed to load the file and all the images ?
A. 2N - 1
B. 2N + 1
C. 1
D. 2
Your Answer: Correct Answer: B Not Attempted Time taken: 00min 00sec Discuss

Your Answer: Correct Answer: D Not Attempted Time taken: 00min 00sec Discuss
Q #5 Multiple Choice Type Award: 1 Penalty: 0.33 Computer Networks
A host is attempting to send data to another host located on a different network. What is the first action the
sending host will take?
A. Drop the data.
B. Create an ARP request to get a MAC address for the default gateway.
C. Create an ARP request to get a MAC address for the receiving host.
D. Send a TCP SYN and wait for the SYN ACK with the IP address of the receiving host
https://gateoverflow.in/quiz/results.php 2/11

2/27/26, 10:11 AM gateoverflow.in/quiz/results.php
Your Answer: Correct Answer: B Not Attempted Time taken: 00min 00sec Discuss
Q #6 Multiple Choice Type Award: 1 Penalty: 0.33 Computer Networks
A TCP/IP Transfer is diagrammed in the exhibit. A window size of three has been negotiated for this transfer.
Which message will be returned from the receiver to the sender as part of this TCP/IP transfer?
A. Send ACK 1-3
B. Send ACK
C. Send ACK 4
D. Send ACK 4-6
Your Answer: Correct Answer: C Not Attempted Time taken: 00min 00sec Discuss
Q #7 Multiple Choice Type Award: 1 Penalty: 0.33 Computer Networks
How does a DHCP server dynamically assign IP address to host?
A. Addresses are allocated after a negotiation between the server and the host to determine the length of
the agreement.
B. Addresses are assigned for a fixed period of time. At the end of period, a new quest for an address must
be made, and another address is then assigned.
C. Addresses are leased to host. A host will usually keep the same address by periodically contacting the
DHCP sever to renew the lease.
D. Addresses are permanently assigned so that the host uses the same address at all times.
Your Answer: Correct Answer: C Not Attempted Time taken: 00min 00sec Discuss
Q #8 Multiple Choice Type Award: 1 Penalty: 0.33 Computer Networks
Which protocol automates all of the following functions for hosts on a network: IP configuration, IP
addresses, subnet masks, default gateways, and DNS server information?
A. UDP
B. HTTP
C. DHCP
D. ARP
Your Answer: Correct Answer: C Not Attempted Time taken: 00min 00sec Discuss

Q #10 Multiple Choice Type Award: 1 Penalty: 0.33 Computer Networks
Stop-And-Wait protocol is highly inefficient when:
A. There is a short distance between source and destination and the transmission rate is high.
B. There is a large distance between source and destination and the transmission rate is high.
C. There is a short distance between source and destination and the transmission rate is low.
D. There is a large distance between source and destination and the transmission rate is low
Your Answer: Correct Answer: B Not Attempted Time taken: 00min 00sec Discuss

D) If a packet arrives with destination address 10101110, it is sent to output 2.

Suppose we want to send data reliably from Los Angeles to New York at , using a pipelined protocol.
Assume that the maximum packet length is 10,000 bits and that the one way delay is 50 ms . How many
distinct sequence numbers must the protocol support? You may assume that packets may be lost, but are
never delivered out-of-order.

A B A
Consider a TCP session from a client to a server . Suppose that the first packet from , following the
"SYN packet" has a sequence number of 200. Suppose that some later packet from A has a sequence number
of 700 , a total IP packet length of 500 bytes and no IP or TCP options. Assume that no packets are lost.
A. 960 bytes have been sent from A so far (including the "current packet").
A

C. The acknowledgment number field in the next packet from B to A should be 1160.
D. The length of the data (payload) in the packet with sequence number 700 is 460 B .
Your Answer: Correct Answer: A;C;D Not Attempted Time taken: 00min 00sec Discuss
Q #14 Numerical Type Award: 2 Penalty: 0 Computer Networks
An application needs to send 100KB of data using a stop-and-wait reliable protocol. The protocol splits the
data into segments that have a 1 KB application data payload. Each segment fits in a single IP packet. The RTT
is 50 ms , there is no packetization delay, and no queueing delay. The protocol uses a fixed retransmission
timeout of 200 ms and has no retransmission limit.
How long will the transmission take, in seconds, if the network does not drop, duplicate or corrupt any
packets? You may assume the connection is established when you start your measurement, so there is no
additional latency from connection setup and consists only of data transmissions. Your answer must be
accurate to two decimal points.
Your Answer: Correct Answer: 5 Not Attempted Time taken: 00min 00sec Discuss
Q #15 Numerical Type Award: 2 Penalty: 0 Computer Networks
A data link uses the Go-Back-N flow control algorithm. A data stream consisting of 1000 packets is
transmitted. 100 of the packets arrive in error. Assuming that all re-transmissions arrive without error, what is
the total number of packets transmitted, including both the original transmissions and re-transmissions.
Assume N=20. Note that we say the ith packet is retransmitted when the receiver discards it due to an error,
and the sender sends it again.
Your Answer: Correct Answer: 3000 Not Attempted Time taken: 00min 00sec Discuss

Q #20 Numerical Type Award: 2 Penalty: 0 Computer Networks
Alyssa P. Hacker has set up eight-node shared medium network running the Carrier Sense Multiple Access
(CSMA) MAC protocol. The maximum data rate of the network is 10 Megabits/s. Including retries, each node
sends traffic according to some unknown random process at an average rate of 1 Megabit/s per node. Alyssa
measures the network's utilization and finds that it is 0.75 . No packets get dropped in the network except
https://gateoverflow.in/quiz/results.php 6/11

due to collisions, and each node's average queue size is 5 packets. Each packet is 10000 bits long. What
fraction of packets sent by the nodes (including retries) experience a collision? (Round off to four decimal
places)
Your Answer: Correct Answer: 0.0625 Not Attempted Time taken: 00min 00sec Discuss
Q #21 Multiple Select Type Award: 2 Penalty: 0 Computer Networks
Consider a sliding window protocol where the receiver sends "ACK k" upon receiving a packet with sequence
number k. Let W denote the window size, and assume the sender's packets start with sequence number 1.
Which of the following is false in a correct implementation of this protocol over an unreliable network?
A. Any new (i.e., previously unsent) packet with sequence number greater than W is sent by the sender if,
and only if, a new (i.e., previously unseen) ACK arrives.
B. The sender will never send more than one packet between the receipt of one ACK and the next.
C. The receiver can discard any new, out-of-order packet it receives after sending an ACK for it.
D. Suppose that no packets or ACKs are lost and no packets are ever retransmitted. Then ACKs will arrive at
the sender in non-decreasing order.
Your Answer: Correct Answer: B;C;D Not Attempted Time taken: 00min 00sec Discuss

Q #24 Multiple Select Type Award: 2 Penalty: 0 Computer Networks
Which of the follow statements about TCP is/are FALSE?
A. TCP's sending window controls the number of bytes that it may send back-to-back.
B. The TCP three-way handshake allows sender and receiver to know the other's initial sequence number.
C. The window field in a TCP header tells a receiver how much space there is in the sender's receive buffer.
D. A TCP sender can overflow the receiver’s buffer.
Your Answer: Correct Answer: C;D Not Attempted Time taken: 00min 00sec Discuss
Q #25 Multiple Select Type Award: 2 Penalty: 0 Computer Networks
Which of the following correctly describe steps in the OSI data encapsulation process?
A. The transport layer divides a data stream into segments and may add reliability and flow control
information.
B. The data link layer adds physical source and destination addresses and an FCS to the segment.
C. Packets are created when the network layer encapsulates a frame with source and destination host
addresses and protocol-related control information.
D. Packets are created when the network layer adds Layer 3 addresses and control information to a
segment.
Your Answer: Correct Answer: A;D Not Attempted Time taken: 00min 00sec Discuss

Refer to the exhibit:
In the network diagram, SW1 and SW2 represent switches, and R1 is a router with interfaces Fa0/0 and
Fa0/1. Four hosts, labeled Host 1, Host 2, Host 3, and Host 4 are connected to these switches. Host 1 and
Host 2 are connected to SW1, while Host 3 and Host 4 are connected to SW2. If Host 1 sends a broadcast
message, and both switches are using their default configurations, what will happen?
A. Hosts 2, 3, and 4 will receive the broadcast.
B. Host 2 and the Fa0/0 interface of R1 will receive the broadcast.
C. Fa0/0 interface of R1 will receive the broadcast.
D. Hosts 1, 2 and the Fa0/0 interface of R1 will receive the broadcast
Your Answer: Correct Answer: B Not Attempted Time taken: 00min 05sec Discuss

Q #9 Multiple Choice Type Award: 1 Penalty: 0.33 CO & Architecture
The MESI protocol (also known as the Illinois protocol) is a widely used cache coherence and memory
coherence protocol. What are the four cache-line states according to the MESI protocol and what are their
respective meanings?
A. Expanded: the cache line was modified (it is double the main memory) and is present in every cache.
Dedicated: the cache line is destined for main memory and is not present in any other cache.
Replicated: the cache line is replicated in main memory and may be present in another cache.
Finite: the cache line contains valid data only in main memory.
B. Modified: the cache line was modified (different from main memory) and is present only in that cache.
Exclusive: the cache line is the same as in main memory and is not present in any other cache.
Shared: the cache line is the same as in main memory and may be present in another cache.
Invalid: the cache line does not contain valid data.
C. Expanded: the cache line was expanded (larger than main memory) and is present in every cache.
Exclusive: the cache line is the same as in main memory and is not present in any other cache.
Replicated: the cache line is replicated in main memory and may be present in another cache.
Finite: the cache line contains valid data only in main memory and the cache is limited.
D. Modified: the cache line was duplicated (different from main memory) and is present in every cache.
Dedicated: the cache line is destined for main memory and is not present in any other cache.
Shared: the cache line is different from main memory and may be present in another cache.
Finite: the cache line contains valid data only in main memory.
Your Answer: Correct Answer: B Not Attempted Time taken: 00min 00sec Discuss

The functionality of the five stages of this pipeline is as follows :

3. Execute (EX) – perform ALU operation, compute jump/branch targets
4. Memory (MEM) – access memory if needed
5. Writeback (WB) – update register file (write back to register file)

An architecture provides an atomic operation fetch-and-increment(x), which atomically returns the current
value of x and then increments it by 1. The atomic operation is non-preemptible.

process starts executing the entry protocol. No process reinitializes inside upon arrival.
Assume that the value of inside never overflows.
// shared atomic counter, initialized once before execution
inside = 0;
// local variable to every process
int a;
do {
a = fetch-and-increment(inside);
} while (a != 0);
/* critical section */
inside = 0;

Q #7 Multiple Choice Type Award: 1 Penalty: 0.33 Databases
In the database systems, the size of a data item is called its granularity. A data item can be a database record,
but it can also be a larger unit such as a whole disk block, or even a smaller unit such as an individual field
(attribute) value of some record in the database.
The granularity of the data items is the portion of the database a data item represents. Granularity(size) of
data items can affect the amount of Concurrency we can achieve with concurrent execution of transactions.
In some database systems, data items are protected by locks to ensure correct behavior in the presence of
concurrency. Locking is said to be "fine-grained" if each lock protects only a few data items; it is said to be

space. Creating the large string in Approach 2 involves string concatenation operations in user space, whereas

approaches?
